{{jalink|Omoikane|Q586088|Omoikane-no-Kami}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|Q462555|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ebisu|Q1129330|Hiruko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Oyamatsumi|Q386563|Oyamatsumi-no-Kami}} * {{jalink|Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-Hohi|Q10940685|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Sukunabikona|Q3275687|Sukunahiko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|White Hare of Inaba|Q2330988|Soto-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|Hiumusubi-no-Kami}} * {{jalink|Kanayamahiko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Sarutahiko|Q3090037|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Emperor Antoku|Q316651}} * {{jalink|Okuninushi|Q276944|Okuninushi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Uwatsutsu-no-o / Nakatsutsu-no-o / Sokotsutsu-no-o}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}} (another candidate shrine is {{jalink|Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{jalink|ubusunagami|Q1392097}}. In {{jalink|1588|Q6757}} ({{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}} 16), {{jalink|Gamo Ujisato|Q906580}} built {{jalink|Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{jalink|Meiji 4|Q11512280}}. On {{jalink|March 23|Q2445}}, {{jalink|1908|Q2056}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{jalink|Gion Festival|Q979873}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{jalink|Gozu Tenno|Q11570247}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{jalink|regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|shikinen senza}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{jalink|Yukan Mie|Q11430176}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{jalink|2016|Q25245}} (Heisei 28), {{jalink|Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|Oshiraishi Arai}} was held on {{jalink|September 11|Q2851}}, and the transfer took place on {{jalink|October 15|Q2919}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{jalink|List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まつさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]] [[Category:need translation]]

{{jalink|Ukanomitama-no-Kami}} |神体 = |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small) / former {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{jalink|shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{jalink|October 15|Q2919}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Matsusaka Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E}}}} }} '''Matsusaka Shrine''' (まつさかじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Matsusaka|Q460829}}, {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former modern rank was {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{jalink|Emperor Ojin|Q317997|Homutawake-no-Mikoto}} ({{jalink|Hachiman deity|Q261637}}) and {{jalink|Ukanomitama-no-Kami}} ({{jalink|Inari deity|Q719665}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{jalink|Amaterasu|Q455602|Amateru Omikami}} * {{jalink|Izanagi|Q813858|Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} / {{jalink|Izanami|Q682306|Izanami-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Takesusanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Tatsuta Taisha|Q246455|Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto / Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Wakahirume-no-Mikoto|Q11596349|Wakahirume-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Takuhatachijihime-no-Mikoto|Q11110848}} * {{jalink|Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|Toyouke Omikami}} * {{jalink|Takamimusubi||Takagi Omikami}} * {{jalink|Amatsuhikone|Q10940228|Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ikutsuhikone|Q11147583|Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Kumanokusubi|Q11298796|Kumanokusubi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Takiribime|Q10932870|Takiribime-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Tagitsuhime|Q10932592|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-tanabatahime-no-kami|Q48750893|Ame-no-tanabatahime-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Omoikane|Q586088|Omoikane-no-Kami}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|Q462555|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ebisu|Q1129330|Hiruko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Oyamatsumi|Q386563|Oyamatsumi-no-Kami}} * {{jalink|Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-Hohi|Q10940685|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Sukunabikona|Q3275687|Sukunahiko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|White Hare of Inaba|Q2330988|Soto-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|Hiumusubi-no-Kami}} * {{jalink|Kanayamahiko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Sarutahiko|Q3090037|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Emperor Antoku|Q316651}} * {{jalink|Okuninushi|Q276944|Okuninushi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Uwatsutsu-no-o / Nakatsutsu-no-o / Sokotsutsu-no-o}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}} (another candidate shrine is {{jalink|Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{jalink|ubusunagami|Q1392097}}. In {{jalink|1588|Q6757}} ({{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}} 16), {{jalink|Gamo Ujisato|Q906580}} built {{jalink|Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{jalink|Meiji 4|Q11512280}}. On {{jalink|March 23|Q2445}}, {{jalink|1908|Q2056}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{jalink|Gion Festival|Q979873}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{jalink|Gozu Tenno|Q11570247}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{jalink|regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|shikinen senza}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{jalink|Yukan Mie|Q11430176}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{jalink|2016|Q25245}} (Heisei 28), {{jalink|Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|Oshiraishi Arai}} was held on {{jalink|September 11|Q2851}}, and the transfer took place on {{jalink|October 15|Q2919}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{jalink|List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まつさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]] [[Category:need translation]]

Haiden |所在地 = {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}{{jalink|Matsusaka|Q460829}}Tonomachi 1445 |位置 = {{Coord|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Homutawake-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Ukanomitama-no-Kami}} |神体 = |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small) / former {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{jalink|shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{jalink|October 15|Q2919}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Matsusaka Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E}}}} }} '''Matsusaka Shrine''' (まつさかじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Matsusaka|Q460829}}, {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former modern rank was {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{jalink|Emperor Ojin|Q317997|Homutawake-no-Mikoto}} ({{jalink|Hachiman deity|Q261637}}) and {{jalink|Ukanomitama-no-Kami}} ({{jalink|Inari deity|Q719665}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{jalink|Amaterasu|Q455602|Amateru Omikami}} * {{jalink|Izanagi|Q813858|Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} / {{jalink|Izanami|Q682306|Izanami-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Takesusanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Tatsuta Taisha|Q246455|Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto / Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Wakahirume-no-Mikoto|Q11596349|Wakahirume-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Takuhatachijihime-no-Mikoto|Q11110848}} * {{jalink|Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|Toyouke Omikami}} * {{jalink|Takamimusubi||Takagi Omikami}} * {{jalink|Amatsuhikone|Q10940228|Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ikutsuhikone|Q11147583|Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Kumanokusubi|Q11298796|Kumanokusubi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Takiribime|Q10932870|Takiribime-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Tagitsuhime|Q10932592|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-tanabatahime-no-kami|Q48750893|Ame-no-tanabatahime-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Omoikane|Q586088|Omoikane-no-Kami}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|Q462555|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ebisu|Q1129330|Hiruko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Oyamatsumi|Q386563|Oyamatsumi-no-Kami}} * {{jalink|Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-Hohi|Q10940685|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Sukunabikona|Q3275687|Sukunahiko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|White Hare of Inaba|Q2330988|Soto-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|Hiumusubi-no-Kami}} * {{jalink|Kanayamahiko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Sarutahiko|Q3090037|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Emperor Antoku|Q316651}} * {{jalink|Okuninushi|Q276944|Okuninushi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Uwatsutsu-no-o / Nakatsutsu-no-o / Sokotsutsu-no-o}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}} (another candidate shrine is {{jalink|Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{jalink|ubusunagami|Q1392097}}. In {{jalink|1588|Q6757}} ({{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}} 16), {{jalink|Gamo Ujisato|Q906580}} built {{jalink|Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{jalink|Meiji 4|Q11512280}}. On {{jalink|March 23|Q2445}}, {{jalink|1908|Q2056}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{jalink|Gion Festival|Q979873}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{jalink|Gozu Tenno|Q11570247}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{jalink|regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|shikinen senza}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{jalink|Yukan Mie|Q11430176}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{jalink|2016|Q25245}} (Heisei 28), {{jalink|Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|Oshiraishi Arai}} was held on {{jalink|September 11|Q2851}}, and the transfer took place on {{jalink|October 15|Q2919}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{jalink|List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まつさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]] [[Category:need translation]]

Haiden |所在地 = {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}{{jalink|Matsusaka|Q460829}}Tonomachi 1445 |位置 = {{Coord|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Homutawake-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Ukanomitama-no-Kami}} |神体 = |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small) / former {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{jalink|shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{jalink|October 15|Q2919}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Matsusaka Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E}}}} }} '''Matsusaka Shrine''' (まつさかじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Matsusaka|Q460829}}, {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former modern rank was {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{jalink|Emperor Ojin|Q317997|Homutawake-no-Mikoto}} ({{jalink|Hachiman deity|Q261637}}) and {{jalink|Ukanomitama-no-Kami}} ({{jalink|Inari deity|Q719665}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{jalink|Amaterasu|Q455602|Amateru Omikami}} * {{jalink|Izanagi|Q813858|Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} / {{jalink|Izanami|Q682306|Izanami-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Takesusanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Tatsuta Taisha|Q246455|Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto / Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Wakahirume-no-Mikoto|Q11596349|Wakahirume-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Takuhatachijihime-no-Mikoto|Q11110848}} * {{jalink|Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|Toyouke Omikami}} * {{jalink|Takamimusubi||Takagi Omikami}} * {{jalink|Amatsuhikone|Q10940228|Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ikutsuhikone|Q11147583|Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Kumanokusubi|Q11298796|Kumanokusubi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Takiribime|Q10932870|Takiribime-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Tagitsuhime|Q10932592|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-tanabatahime-no-kami|Q48750893|Ame-no-tanabatahime-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Omoikane|Q586088|Omoikane-no-Kami}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|Q462555|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ebisu|Q1129330|Hiruko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Oyamatsumi|Q386563|Oyamatsumi-no-Kami}} * {{jalink|Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-Hohi|Q10940685|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Sukunabikona|Q3275687|Sukunahiko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|White Hare of Inaba|Q2330988|Soto-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|Hiumusubi-no-Kami}} * {{jalink|Kanayamahiko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Sarutahiko|Q3090037|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Emperor Antoku|Q316651}} * {{jalink|Okuninushi|Q276944|Okuninushi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Uwatsutsu-no-o / Nakatsutsu-no-o / Sokotsutsu-no-o}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}} (another candidate shrine is {{jalink|Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{jalink|ubusunagami|Q1392097}}. In {{jalink|1588|Q6757}} ({{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}} 16), {{jalink|Gamo Ujisato|Q906580}} built {{jalink|Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{jalink|Meiji 4|Q11512280}}. On {{jalink|March 23|Q2445}}, {{jalink|1908|Q2056}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{jalink|Gion Festival|Q979873}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{jalink|Gozu Tenno|Q11570247}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{jalink|regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|shikinen senza}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{jalink|Yukan Mie|Q11430176}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{jalink|2016|Q25245}} (Heisei 28), {{jalink|Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|Oshiraishi Arai}} was held on {{jalink|September 11|Q2851}}, and the transfer took place on {{jalink|October 15|Q2919}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{jalink|List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まつさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]]

{{jalink|Ukanomitama-no-Kami}} |神体 = |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small) / former {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{jalink|shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{jalink|October 15|Q2919}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Matsusaka Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E}}}} }} '''Matsusaka Shrine''' (まつさかじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Matsusaka|Q460829}}, {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former modern rank was {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{jalink|Emperor Ojin|Q317997|Homutawake-no-Mikoto}} ({{jalink|Hachiman deity|Q261637}}) and {{jalink|Ukanomitama-no-Kami}} ({{jalink|Inari deity|Q719665}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{jalink|Amaterasu|Q455602|Amateru Omikami}} * {{jalink|Izanagi|Q813858|Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} / {{jalink|Izanami|Q682306|Izanami-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Takesusanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Tatsuta Taisha|Q246455|Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto / Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Wakahirume-no-Mikoto|Q11596349|Wakahirume-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Takuhatachijihime-no-Mikoto|Q11110848}} * {{jalink|Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|Toyouke Omikami}} * {{jalink|Takamimusubi||Takagi Omikami}} * {{jalink|Amatsuhikone|Q10940228|Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ikutsuhikone|Q11147583|Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Kumanokusubi|Q11298796|Kumanokusubi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Takiribime|Q10932870|Takiribime-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Tagitsuhime|Q10932592|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-tanabatahime-no-kami|Q48750893|Ame-no-tanabatahime-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Omoikane|Q586088|Omoikane-no-Kami}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|Q462555|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ebisu|Q1129330|Hiruko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Oyamatsumi|Q386563|Oyamatsumi-no-Kami}} * {{jalink|Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-Hohi|Q10940685|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Sukunabikona|Q3275687|Sukunahiko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|White Hare of Inaba|Q2330988|Soto-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|Hiumusubi-no-Kami}} * {{jalink|Kanayamahiko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Sarutahiko|Q3090037|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Emperor Antoku|Q316651}} * {{jalink|Okuninushi|Q276944|Okuninushi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Uwatsutsu-no-o / Nakatsutsu-no-o / Sokotsutsu-no-o}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}} (another candidate shrine is {{jalink|Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{jalink|ubusunagami|Q1392097}}. In {{jalink|1588|Q6757}} ({{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}} 16), {{jalink|Gamo Ujisato|Q906580}} built {{jalink|Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{jalink|Meiji 4|Q11512280}}. On {{jalink|March 23|Q2445}}, {{jalink|1908|Q2056}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{jalink|Gion Festival|Q979873}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{jalink|Gozu Tenno|Q11570247}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{jalink|regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|shikinen senza}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{jalink|Yukan Mie|Q11430176}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{jalink|2016|Q25245}} (Heisei 28), {{jalink|Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|Oshiraishi Arai}} was held on {{jalink|September 11|Q2851}}, and the transfer took place on {{jalink|October 15|Q2919}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{jalink|List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まつさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

{{jalink|Ukanomitama-no-Kami}} |神体 = |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small) / former {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{jalink|shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{jalink|October 15|Q2919}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Matsusaka Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E}}}} }} '''Matsusaka Shrine''' (まつさかじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Matsusaka|Q460829}}, {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former modern rank was {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{jalink|Emperor Ojin|Q317997|Homutawake-no-Mikoto}} ({{jalink|Hachiman deity|Q261637}}) and {{jalink|Ukanomitama-no-Kami}} ({{jalink|Inari deity|Q719665}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{jalink|Amaterasu|Q455602|Amateru Omikami}} * {{jalink|Izanagi|Q813858|Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} / {{jalink|Izanami|Q682306|Izanami-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Takesusanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Tatsuta Taisha|Q246455|Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto / Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Wakahirume-no-Mikoto|Q11596349|Wakahirume-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Takuhatachijihime-no-Mikoto|Q11110848}} * {{jalink|Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|Toyouke Omikami}} * {{jalink|Takamimusubi||Takagi Omikami}} * {{jalink|Amatsuhikone|Q10940228|Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ikutsuhikone|Q11147583|Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Kumanokusubi|Q11298796|Kumanokusubi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Takiribime|Q10932870|Takiribime-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Tagitsuhime|Q10932592|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-tanabatahime-no-kami|Q48750893|Ame-no-tanabatahime-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Omoikane|Q586088|Omoikane-no-Kami}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|Q462555|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ebisu|Q1129330|Hiruko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Oyamatsumi|Q386563|Oyamatsumi-no-Kami}} * {{jalink|Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-Hohi|Q10940685|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Sukunabikona|Q3275687|Sukunahiko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|White Hare of Inaba|Q2330988|Soto-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|Hiumusubi-no-Kami}} * {{jalink|Kanayamahiko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Sarutahiko|Q3090037|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Emperor Antoku|Q316651}} * {{jalink|Okuninushi|Q276944|Okuninushi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Uwatsutsu-no-o / Nakatsutsu-no-o / Sokotsutsu-no-o}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}} (another candidate shrine is {{jalink|Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{jalink|ubusunagami|Q1392097}}. In {{jalink|1588|Q6757}} ({{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}} 16), {{jalink|Gamo Ujisato|Q906580}} built {{jalink|Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{jalink|Meiji 4|Q11512280}}. On {{jalink|March 23|Q2445}}, {{jalink|1908|Q2056}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{jalink|Gion Festival|Q979873}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{jalink|Gozu Tenno|Q11570247}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{jalink|regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|shikinen senza}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{jalink|Yukan Mie|Q11430176}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{jalink|2016|Q25245}} (Heisei 28), {{jalink|Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|Oshiraishi Arai}} was held on {{jalink|September 11|Q2851}}, and the transfer took place on {{jalink|October 15|Q2919}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{jalink|List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まつさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

It is a {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}, and its former modern rank was {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto}} ({{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637}}) and {{ill||lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amateru Omikami}} * {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} / {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Takesusanoo-no-Mikoto}} * History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まつさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}, and its former modern rank was {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto}} ({{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637}}) and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amateru Omikami}} * History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まつさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}, and its former modern rank was {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto}} ({{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637}}) and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amateru Omikami}} * {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} / {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Takesusanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Tatsuta Taisha|Q246455|WD=Q246455|lt=Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto / Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Wakahirume-no-Mikoto|Q11596349|WD=Q11596349|lt=Wakahirume-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Takuhatachijihime-no-Mikoto|Q11110848|WD=Q11110848}} * {{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Omikami}} * {{ill|lt=Takamimusubi||WD=|lt=Takagi Omikami}} * {{ill|lt=Amatsuhikone|Q10940228|WD=Q10940228|lt=Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ikutsuhikone|Q11147583|WD=Q11147583|lt=Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Kumanokusubi|Q11298796|WD=Q11298796|lt=Kumanokusubi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Takiribime|Q10932870|WD=Q10932870|lt=Takiribime-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Tagitsuhime|Q10932592|WD=Q10932592|lt=Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-tanabatahime-no-kami|Q48750893|WD=Q48750893|lt=Ame-no-tanabatahime-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Omoikane|Q586088|WD=Q586088|lt=Omoikane-no-Kami}} * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi|Q462555|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ebisu|Q1129330|WD=Q1129330|lt=Hiruko-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Oyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Oyamatsumi-no-Kami}} * {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Hohi|Q10940685|WD=Q10940685|lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahiko-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=White Hare of Inaba|Q2330988|WD=Q2330988|lt=Soto-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Hiumusubi-no-Kami}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Sarutahiko|Q3090037|WD=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Antoku|Q316651|WD=Q316651}} * {{ill|lt=Okuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Okuninushi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o / Nakatsutsu-no-o / Sokotsutsu-no-o}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まつさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}, and its former modern rank was {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto}} ({{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637}}) and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amateru Omikami}} * {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} / {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Takesusanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Tatsuta Taisha|Q246455|WD=Q246455|lt=Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto / Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Wakahirume-no-Mikoto|Q11596349|WD=Q11596349|lt=Wakahirume-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Takuhatachijihime-no-Mikoto|Q11110848|WD=Q11110848}} * {{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Omikami}} * {{ill|lt=Takamimusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=|lt=Takagi Omikami}} * History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まつさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}, and its former modern rank was {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto}} ({{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637}}) and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amateru Omikami}} * {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} / {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Takesusanoo-no-Mikoto}} * History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まつさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former modern rank was {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto}} ({{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637}}) and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amateru Omikami}} * {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} / {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Takesusanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Tatsuta Taisha|Q246455|WD=Q246455|lt=Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto / Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Wakahirume-no-Mikoto|Q11596349|WD=Q11596349|lt=Wakahirume-no-Mikoto}} * History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まつさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former modern rank was {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto}} ({{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637}}) and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amateru Omikami}} * {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} / {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Takesusanoo-no-Mikoto}} * History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まつさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まつさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former modern rank was {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto}} ({{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637}}) and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amateru Omikami}} * {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} / {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Takesusanoo-no-Mikoto}} * Sokotsutsu-no-o}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まつさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former modern rank was {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto}} ({{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637}}) and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amateru Omikami}} * {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} / {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Takesusanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Tatsuta Taisha|Q246455|WD=Q246455|lt=Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto / Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Wakahirume-no-Mikoto|Q11596349|WD=Q11596349|lt=Wakahirume-no-Mikoto}} * Sokotsutsu-no-o}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まつさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former modern rank was {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto}} ({{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637}}) and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amateru Omikami}} * {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} / {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Takesusanoo-no-Mikoto}} *
