Sokotsutsu-no-o}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まつさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former modern rank was {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto}} ({{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637}}) and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amateru Omikami}} * {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} / {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Takesusanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o / Nakatsutsu-no-o / Sokotsutsu-no-o}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まつさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

* {{ill|lt=Oyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Oyamatsumi-no-Kami}} * {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto}} * Inaba|Q2330988|WD=Q2330988|lt=Soto-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Hiumusubi-no-Kami}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Sarutahiko|Q3090037|WD=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Antoku|Q316651|WD=Q316651}} * {{ill|WD=Q276944|lt=Okuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Okuninushi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o / Nakatsutsu-no-o / Sokotsutsu-no-o}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まつさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small) / former {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Matsusaka Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E}}}} }} '''Matsusaka Shrine''' (まつさかじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Matsusaka|Q460829|WD=Q460829|1=Matsusaka}}, {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former modern rank was {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} ({{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}}) and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665|1=Inari Ōkami}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amateru Omikami|1=Amaterasu}} * {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}} / {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}} * Nakatsutsu-no-o / Sokotsutsu-no-o|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193|1=Kanbe Kotachi Shinmeisha}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757|1=1588}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580|1=Gamō Ujisato}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280|1=Meiji – year 4}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445|1=March 23}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873|1=Gion Matsuri}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza|1=Sengū}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176|1=Yukan Mie}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245|1=2016}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai|1=Oshiraishimochi}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851|1=September 11}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540|1=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まつさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former modern rank was {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} ({{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}}) and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665|1=Inari Ōkami}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amateru Omikami|1=Amaterasu}} * Nakatsutsu-no-o / Sokotsutsu-no-o|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193|1=Kanbe Kotachi Shinmeisha}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757|1=1588}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580|1=Gamō Ujisato}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280|1=Meiji – year 4}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445|1=March 23}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873|1=Gion Matsuri}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza|1=Sengū}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176|1=Yukan Mie}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245|1=2016}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai|1=Oshiraishimochi}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851|1=September 11}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540|1=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まつさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former modern rank was {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} ({{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}}) and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665|1=Inari Ōkami}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amateru Omikami|1=Amaterasu}} * Nakatsutsu-no-o / Sokotsutsu-no-o|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193|1=Kanbe Kotachi Shinmeisha}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757|1=1588}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580|1=Gamō Ujisato}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280|1=Meiji – year 4}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445|1=March 23}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873|1=Gion Matsuri}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza|1=Sengū}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176|1=Yukan Mie}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245|1=2016}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai|1=Oshiraishimochi}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851|1=September 11}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540|1=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まつさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small) / former {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Matsusaka Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E}}}} }} '''Matsusaka Shrine''' (まつさかじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Matsusaka|Q460829|WD=Q460829|1=Matsusaka}}, {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former modern rank was {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} ({{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}}) and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665|1=Inari Ōkami}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amateru Omikami|1=Amaterasu}} * {{ill|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o / Nakatsutsu-no-o / Sokotsutsu-no-o|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193|1=Kanbe Kotachi Shinmeisha}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757|1=1588}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580|1=Gamō Ujisato}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280|1=Meiji – year 4}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445|1=March 23}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873|1=Gion Matsuri}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza|1=Sengū}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176|1=Yukan Mie}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245|1=2016}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai|1=Oshiraishimochi}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851|1=September 11}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540|1=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Matsusaka|Q460829|WD=Q460829|1=Matsusaka}}Tonomachi 1445 |位置 = {{Coord|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small) / former {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Matsusaka Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E}}}} }} '''Matsusaka Shrine''' (まつさかじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Matsusaka|Q460829|WD=Q460829|1=Matsusaka}}, {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former modern rank was {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} ({{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}}) and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665|1=Inari Ōkami}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amateru Omikami|1=Amaterasu}} * {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}} / {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Takesusanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Tatsuta Taisha|Q246455|WD=Q246455|lt=Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto / Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|1=Tatsuta Taisha}} * {{ill|lt=Wakahirume-no-Mikoto|Q11596349|WD=Q11596349|lt=Wakahirume-no-Mikoto|1=Wakahiru-me}} * {{ill|lt=Takuhatachijihime-no-Mikoto|Q11110848|WD=Q11110848|1=Takuhadachiji-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Omikami|1=Toyouke-hime}} * {{ill|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi|UNKNOWN|lt=Takagi Omikami|1=Takamimusubi}} * {{ill|lt=Amatsuhikone|Q10940228|WD=Q10940228|lt=Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto|1=Amatsuhikone}} * {{ill|lt=Ikutsuhikone|Q11147583|WD=Q11147583|lt=Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto|1=Ikutsuhikone}} * {{ill|lt=Kumanokusubi|Q11298796|WD=Q11298796|lt=Kumanokusubi-no-Mikoto|1=Kumanokusubi}} * {{ill|lt=Takiribime|Q10932870|WD=Q10932870|lt=Takiribime-no-Mikoto|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|lt=Tagitsuhime|Q10932592|WD=Q10932592|lt=Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|1=Tagitsuhime}} * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-tanabatahime-no-kami|Q48750893|WD=Q48750893|lt=Ame-no-tanabatahime-no-Mikoto|1=Tanabatabime-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Omoikane|Q586088|WD=Q586088|lt=Omoikane-no-Kami|1=Omoikane}} * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi|Q462555|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}} * {{ill|lt=Ebisu|Q1129330|WD=Q1129330|lt=Hiruko-no-Mikoto|1=Ebisu (mythology)}} * {{ill|lt=Oyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Oyamatsumi-no-Kami|1=Ōyamatsumi}} * {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}} * {{ill|WD=Q10940685|lt=Ame-no-Hohi|Q10940685|WD=Q10940685|WD=Q10940685|lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|1=Ame no Hohi}} * {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahiko-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Sarutahiko|Q3090037|WD=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|1=Sarutahiko Ōkami}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Antoku|Q316651|WD=Q316651|1=Emperor Antoku}} * {{ill|WD=Q276944|lt=Okuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Okuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}} * {{ill|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o / Nakatsutsu-no-o / Sokotsutsu-no-o|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193|1=Kanbe Kotachi Shinmeisha}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757|1=1588}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580|1=Gamō Ujisato}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280|1=Meiji – year 4}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445|1=March 23}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873|1=Gion Matsuri}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza|1=Sengū}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176|1=Yukan Mie}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245|1=2016}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai|1=Oshiraishimochi}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851|1=September 11}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540|1=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:三重県の旧郷社]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small) / former {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Matsusaka Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E}}}} }} '''Matsusaka Shrine''' (まつさかじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Matsusaka|Q460829|WD=Q460829|1=Matsusaka}}, {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former modern rank was {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} ({{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}}) and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665|1=Inari Ōkami}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amateru Omikami|1=Amaterasu}} * {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}} / {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Takesusanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Tatsuta Taisha|Q246455|WD=Q246455|lt=Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto / Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|1=Tatsuta Taisha}} * {{ill|lt=Wakahirume-no-Mikoto|Q11596349|WD=Q11596349|lt=Wakahirume-no-Mikoto|1=Wakahiru-me}} * {{ill|lt=Takuhatachijihime-no-Mikoto|Q11110848|WD=Q11110848|1=Takuhadachiji-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Omikami|1=Toyouke-hime}} * {{ill|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi|UNKNOWN|lt=Takagi Omikami|1=Takamimusubi}} * {{ill|lt=Amatsuhikone|Q10940228|WD=Q10940228|lt=Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto|1=Amatsuhikone}} * {{ill|lt=Ikutsuhikone|Q11147583|WD=Q11147583|lt=Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto|1=Ikutsuhikone}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Sarutahiko|Q3090037|WD=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|1=Sarutahiko Ōkami}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Antoku|Q316651|WD=Q316651|1=Emperor {{ill|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o / Nakatsutsu-no-o / Sokotsutsu-no-o|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193|1=Kanbe Kotachi Shinmeisha}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757|1=1588}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580|1=Gamō Ujisato}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280|1=Meiji – year 4}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445|1=March 23}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873|1=Gion Matsuri}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza|1=Sengū}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176|1=Yukan Mie}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245|1=2016}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai|1=Oshiraishimochi}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851|1=September 11}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540|1=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

Nakatsutsu-no-o / Sokotsutsu-no-o|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193|1=Kanbe Kotachi Shinmeisha}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757|1=1588}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580|1=Gamō Ujisato}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280|1=Meiji – year 4}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445|1=March 23}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873|1=Gion Matsuri}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza|1=Sengū}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176|1=Yukan Mie}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245|1=2016}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai|1=Oshiraishimochi}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851|1=September 11}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540|1=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:松阪市の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small) / former {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Matsusaka Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E}}}} }} '''Matsusaka Shrine''' (まつさかじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Matsusaka|Q460829|WD=Q460829|1=Matsusaka}}, {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former modern rank was {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} ({{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}}) and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665|1=Inari Ōkami}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amateru Omikami|1=Amaterasu}} * {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}} / {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Takesusanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Tatsuta Taisha|Q246455|WD=Q246455|lt=Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto / Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|1=Tatsuta Taisha}} * {{ill|lt=Wakahirume-no-Mikoto|Q11596349|WD=Q11596349|lt=Wakahirume-no-Mikoto|1=Wakahiru-me}} * {{ill|lt=Takuhatachijihime-no-Mikoto|Q11110848|WD=Q11110848|1=Takuhadachiji-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Omikami|1=Toyouke-hime}} * {{ill|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi|UNKNOWN|lt=Takagi Omikami|1=Takamimusubi}} * {{ill|lt=Amatsuhikone|Q10940228|WD=Q10940228|lt=Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto|1=Amatsuhikone}} * {{ill|lt=Ikutsuhikone|Q11147583|WD=Q11147583|lt=Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto|1=Ikutsuhikone}} * {{ill|lt=Kumanokusubi|Q11298796|WD=Q11298796|lt=Kumanokusubi-no-Mikoto|1=Kumanokusubi}} * {{ill|lt=Takiribime|Q10932870|WD=Q10932870|lt=Takiribime-no-Mikoto|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|lt=Tagitsuhime|Q10932592|WD=Q10932592|lt=Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|1=Tagitsuhime}} * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-tanabatahime-no-kami|Q48750893|WD=Q48750893|lt=Ame-no-tanabatahime-no-Mikoto|1=Tanabatabime-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Omoikane|Q586088|WD=Q586088|lt=Omoikane-no-Kami|1=Omoikane}} * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi|Q462555|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}} * {{ill|lt=Ebisu|Q1129330|WD=Q1129330|lt=Hiruko-no-Mikoto|1=Ebisu (mythology)}} * {{ill|lt=Oyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Oyamatsumi-no-Kami|1=Ōyamatsumi}} * {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}} * {{ill|WD=Q10940685|lt=Ame-no-Hohi|Q10940685|WD=Q10940685|WD=Q10940685|lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|1=Ame no Hohi}} * {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahiko-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Sarutahiko|Q3090037|WD=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|1=Sarutahiko Ōkami}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Antoku|Q316651|WD=Q316651|1=Emperor Antoku}} * {{ill|WD=Q276944|lt=Okuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Okuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}} * {{ill|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o / Nakatsutsu-no-o / Sokotsutsu-no-o|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193|1=Kanbe Kotachi Shinmeisha}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757|1=1588}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580|1=Gamō Ujisato}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280|1=Meiji – year 4}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445|1=March 23}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873|1=Gion Matsuri}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza|1=Sengū}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176|1=Yukan Mie}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245|1=2016}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai|1=Oshiraishimochi}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851|1=September 11}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540|1=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:松阪市の歴史]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:Buildings in Matsusaka, Mie]][[Category:qq]]

It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former modern rank was {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} ({{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}}) and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665|1=Inari Ōkami}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amateru Omikami|1=Amaterasu}} * {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}} / {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}} * Nakatsutsu-no-o / Sokotsutsu-no-o|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193|1=Kanbe Kotachi Shinmeisha}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757|1=1588}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580|1=Gamō Ujisato}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280|1=Meiji – year 4}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445|1=March 23}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873|1=Gion Matsuri}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza|1=Sengū}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176|1=Yukan Mie}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245|1=2016}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai|1=Oshiraishimochi}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851|1=September 11}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540|1=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Matsusaka, Mie]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:Buildings in Matsusaka, Mie]][[Category:qq]]

It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former modern rank was {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} ({{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}}) and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665|1=Inari Ōkami}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amateru Omikami|1=Amaterasu}} * Nakatsutsu-no-o / Sokotsutsu-no-o|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193|1=Kanbe Kotachi Shinmeisha}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757|1=1588}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580|1=Gamō Ujisato}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280|1=Meiji – year 4}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445|1=March 23}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873|1=Gion Matsuri}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza|1=Sengū}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176|1=Yukan Mie}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245|1=2016}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai|1=Oshiraishimochi}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851|1=September 11}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540|1=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Matsusaka, Mie]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:Buildings in Matsusaka, Mie]]

{{ill|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o / Nakatsutsu-no-o / Sokotsutsu-no-o|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193|1=Kanbe Kotachi Shinmeisha}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757|1=1588}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580|1=Gamō Ujisato}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280|1=Meiji – year 4}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445|1=March 23}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873|1=Gion Matsuri}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza|1=Sengū}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176|1=Yukan Mie}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245|1=2016}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai|1=Oshiraishimochi}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851|1=September 11}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540|1=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Matsusaka, Mie]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:Buildings in Matsusaka, Mie]]

{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small) / former {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Matsusaka Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|34|25.4|N|136|31|29.2|E}}}} }} '''Matsusaka Shrine''' (まつさかじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Matsusaka|Q460829|WD=Q460829|1=Matsusaka}}, {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former modern rank was {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} ({{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}}) and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665|1=Inari Ōkami}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amateru Omikami|1=Amaterasu}} * {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}} / {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}} * Nakatsutsu-no-o / Sokotsutsu-no-o|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|WD=Q11590193|1=Kanbe Kotachi Shinmeisha}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|WD=Q6757|1=1588}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580|1=Gamō Ujisato}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|WD=Q11512280|1=Meiji – year 4}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|WD=Q2445|1=March 23}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873|1=Gion Matsuri}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza|1=Sengū}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|WD=Q11430176|1=Yukan Mie}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|WD=Q25245|1=2016}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|WD=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai|1=Oshiraishimochi}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|WD=Q2851|1=September 11}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|WD=Q48761540|1=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Matsusaka, Mie]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsusaka, Mie]]

It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}}, and its former modern rank was {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === Its principal deities are {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|qid=Q317997|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} ({{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|qid=Q261637|1=Hachiman}}) and {{ill|UNKNOWN|qid=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|qid=Q719665|1=Inari Ōkami}}). Through shrine mergers in the Meiji period, the following deities are also enshrined. {{colbegin|2}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|qid=Q455602|lt=Amateru Omikami|1=Amaterasu}} * {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|qid=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}} / {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}} * Nakatsutsu-no-o / Sokotsutsu-no-o|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} {{colend}} === History === It is regarded as a successor shrine of "Oi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} (another candidate shrine is {{ill|lt=Kambe Kamidate Shinmeisha|Q11590193|qid=Q11590193|1=Kanbe Kotachi Shinmeisha}} in Shimomura-cho, Matsusaka). Its founding date is unknown, but it has stood on this site since ancient times and was revered by the Iitaka clan, district governors, as their {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|qid=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}}. In {{ill|lt=1588|Q6757|qid=Q6757|1=1588}} ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|qid=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 16), {{ill|lt=Gamo Ujisato|Q906580|qid=Q906580|1=Gamō Ujisato}} built {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|qid=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}} in this shrine forest (Oi no Mori), made the shrine the castle guardian, and enshrined the Hachiman deity together with it. From then through the Edo period it was called "Ojohachiman" (Castle Hachiman). In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|qid=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}, when Yoshida Shigekatsu repaired Matsusaka Castle, Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto was additionally enshrined. In the early Meiji period, it was identified with the Shikinaisha Oi Shrine and renamed from Ojohachiman to Oi Shrine, then ranked as a gosha in July {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|qid=Q11512280|1=Meiji – year 4}}. On {{ill|lt=March 23|Q2445|qid=Q2445|1=March 23}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|qid=Q2056|1=1908}} (Meiji 41), deities from 17 nearby shrines (33 deities in total) were merged and it was renamed Matsusaka Shrine. === Festivals === In mid-July, this shrine, Hino-cho Yakumo Shrine, and Honmachi Mikuriya Shrine hold a {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|qid=Q979873|1=Gion Matsuri}}. Originally this was the festival of Uomachi Uryu Shrine and Nishimachi Yaegaki Shrine, both later merged into this shrine. These shrines were called the "Four Heavenly Kings" as shrines enshrining Matsusaka's {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|qid=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}}. === Regular 20-year transfer === A {{ill|lt=regular shrine transfer|Q2271023|qid=Q2271023|lt=shikinen senza|1=Sengū}} is held every 20 years{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|title=Final rite "Oshiraishi Arai" held at Matsusaka Shrine; parishioners prepare for once-every-20-years shikinen sengu next month on the 15th|publisher={{ill|lt=Yukan Mie|Q11430176|qid=Q11430176|1=Yukan Mie}}|date=2016-09-12|accessdate=2016-10-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010144610/http://www.yukanmie.com/news/1544/|archivedate=2016-10-10|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2017-10}}. In {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|qid=Q25245|1=2016}} (Heisei 28), {{ill|lt=Oshiraishi washing|Q17225061|qid=Q17225061|lt=Oshiraishi Arai|1=Oshiraishimochi}} was held on {{ill|lt=September 11|Q2851|qid=Q2851|1=September 11}}, and the transfer took place on {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|qid=Q2919|1=October 15}}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture|Q48761540|qid=Q48761540|1=List of shrines in Mie Prefecture}} === External links === * [http://www.matsusakajinjya.com/ Matsusaka Shrine] (official website) {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Matsusaka Shrine]][[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Matsusaka, Mie]] [[Category:2016年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsusaka, Mie]]
